hypothesis: Incorporating a Dirichlet-process-based noise schedule into PCDiff's forward diffusion process—replacing the standard Gaussian noise with noise sampled from a Dirichlet distribution parameterized to respect the simplex geometry of composite compositions—will simultaneously eliminate the need for post-hoc regularization penalties while achieving higher validity, density, and coverage scores than the current penalty-based constraint enforcement, because the generative dynamics will inherently remain on the compositional simplex at every diffusion timestep rather than requiring external correction terms to project back onto the feasible space.
